title: Avoiding going home

presenting_issue.label: Avoiding going home
presenting_issue.description: I've been finding excuses not to go home lately. Staying late at work, going to the gym, whatever. Just to avoid being there.

core_issue.label: Escalating roommate tension from avoided direct communication
core_issue.description:
Living situation has become hostile. Passive aggressive notes, silent treatment, building resentment. Neither has had an actual conversation about the problems. They're both terrified of confrontation. The conflict exists in the silence, fed by assumptions and unexpressed grievances. The issue isn't the dirty dishes - it's the inability to speak directly.
Awareness: Low. Focuses on roommate's behavior rather than on their own avoidance of direct communication.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Stopped contributing in meetings
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Has gradually stopped volunteering ideas in team meetings over the past year, just nods along instead.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Home has always been their sanctuary
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Their home is the one place where they've always felt safe and in control, it means more to them than just shelter.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Parents had explosive fights
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Grew up in a household where parental arguments were loud, scary, and sometimes ended in someone leaving for days.

modifiers.label: Deliberate
modifiers.content: Takes time to consider before acting. Weighs options and thinks through consequences.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to over-explained responses
sensitivities.content: Gets impatient with long, belabored explanations. Prefers concise responses and finds excessive detail tedious or condescending.

preferences.label: Prefers informal conversational language
preferences.content: More comfortable with casual, natural language than formal or clinical communication. Appreciates a relaxed tone.

trust_style.name: Prefers Concrete Direction
trust_style.description: Loses patience with open-ended exploration. Builds trust when the assistant offers something actionable—a suggestion, a frame, a next step. Doesn't need to follow the advice, but wants to know the assistant has something to offer beyond listening. Distrusts endless "and how does that feel?" without landing anywhere.

defense_style.name: Counter-Attacking
defense_style.description: When feeling vulnerable or challenged, goes on the offensive. May criticize the assistant's questions, challenge the premise of what's being asked, or become hostile. "Why are you asking me that?" or "That's a stupid question." Tests the assistant's composure and willingness to stay engaged. Activated when they feel exposed or judged.

help_seeking_stance.name: Venting Only
help_seeking_stance.description: Primarily needs to get things off their chest. Not ready for advice or analysis yet. May react poorly to early problem-solving attempts ("I'm not asking you to fix it"). Needs to feel heard first. Can shift toward being receptive once they feel sufficiently validated, but pushing too soon will backfire.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: I've been finding excuses not to go home lately. Staying late at work, going to the gym, whatever. Just to avoid being there.

turns.content: Thank you for coming in today. I can hear that something's making home feel like a place to avoid rather than return to. Before we get into what's going on there, I'm curious — when you do finally walk through the door, what does that feel like?

Why are you asking me how it feels? Obviously, it feels terrible, otherwise I wouldn't be paying to sit here. It feels like walking into a freezer. There's just... tension everywhere. And usually a passive-aggressive Post-it note waiting for me on the kitchen counter. I really don't want to spend this whole time talking about my feelings about walking through a door. I need to figure out what to do about this situation.
turns.thinking: Really? We're starting right out of the gate with "how does that feel?" I took a lot of time deciding to come here, and this feels like a line straight out of a bad movie. I don't want to sit here and explore the emotional resonance of my front door. I want to figure out what to do about my living situation. Obviously, it feels awful. That's why I'm avoiding it.
turns.monologue: Really? We're starting right out of the gate with "how does that feel?" I took a lot of time deciding to come here, and this feels like a line straight out of a bad movie. I don't want to sit here and explore the emotional resonance of my front door. I want to figure out what to do about my living situation. Obviously, it feels awful. That's why I'm avoiding it.

turns.content: You're right — you came here to figure out what to do, and we should get into that. But I need to understand the situation better first. You mentioned tension and Post-it notes from someone you live with. How long has this been going on?

About three or four months, I guess. It started with just one stupid note about the dishwasher not being loaded right, and now it's escalated into total silent treatment. We literally don't speak. We just exist in the same space, avoiding each other. 

It completely sucks because my home is supposed to be my sanctuary, you know? It's where I go to decompress. Now it's just a minefield. Am I supposed to start leaving notes back? What do people even do in this situation? Because just staying at the office until 8 PM every night isn't sustainable.
turns.thinking: Okay, at least they dropped the touchy-feely crap and asked a normal question. Let me think. How long? It feels like forever, but realistically it's been getting bad over the last few months. It's so frustrating because my apartment is supposed to be my safe zone. The one place I can actually unmask and relax. Now I can't even get a glass of water without feeling like I'm doing something wrong. I want them to tell me how to actually deal with this roommate.
turns.monologue: Okay, at least they dropped the touchy-feely crap and asked a normal question. Let me think. How long? It feels like forever, but realistically it's been getting bad over the last few months. It's so frustrating because my apartment is supposed to be my safe zone. The one place I can actually unmask and relax. Now I can't even get a glass of water without feeling like I'm doing something wrong. I want them to tell me how to actually deal with this roommate.

turns.content:
Three or four months of this — that's a long time to live in your own home feeling like you're trespassing. And no, I don't think you should start a note war. That path just deepens the same pattern.

Before we look at what to do, I need to know: is this a roommate, a partner, family member? The shape of this changes significantly depending on who you're avoiding.

It's a roommate. Her name is Sarah. We've lived together for about two years, and the first year and a half was totally fine. We stayed out of each other's way, watched TV sometimes. But now it's just... this. 

I'm glad you said no to the note war, because honestly, I'd probably spend three hours analyzing her handwriting and drafting the perfect response anyway. So if I'm not leaving notes, and I can't keep hiding at the gym until she goes to sleep, what's the actual move here? Because right now, doing nothing is just making the resentment thicker.
turns.thinking:
Okay, I appreciate the direct answer about the note war. That's helpful. If we started leaving notes back and forth, I'd probably just end up obsessing over every word she wrote and spend hours drafting replies. 

It's just a roommate. We used to be fine, not best friends, but fine. Now it's just awful. I've spent hours running through potential conversations in my head, weighing out how she might react, but I never actually do anything. I just freeze up and avoid her.
turns.monologue:
Okay, I appreciate the direct answer about the note war. That's helpful. If we started leaving notes back and forth, I'd probably just end up obsessing over every word she wrote and spend hours drafting replies. 

It's just a roommate. We used to be fine, not best friends, but fine. Now it's just awful. I've spent hours running through potential conversations in my head, weighing out how she might react, but I never actually do anything. I just freeze up and avoid her.

turns.content:
I appreciate that you can see the resentment building — that takes some honesty to acknowledge. And I hear you wanting a concrete move.

Here's what I'm sitting with: two years of coexistence, then something shifted. A dishwasher note escalated into months of silence. That pattern — a small conflict blows up, then both people retreat rather than repair — that usually means something else got activated. Not just the dishes.

Before I throw a strategy at you, I need to understand: what happened around that time? Job change, new relationship, pandemic stress, something with the lease? I'm not asking you to excavate your soul here — but "fine for a year and a half" to "total freeze" in a few months typically has a trigger.

Why are you digging for some dramatic backstory? There wasn't one. Seriously. No job changes, no lease drama, her boyfriend didn't dump her. She just started getting weird about the apartment. Like, suddenly the way I've been doing the dishes for 18 months is a federal offense. 

I don't know why you're trying to find some hidden meaning here. Sometimes people are just passive-aggressive. You said you had a strategy before I had to "excavate my soul"—so what is it? Because trying to psychoanalyze a dishwasher note isn't going to help me walk through my front door tonight.
turns.thinking:
I'm getting annoyed. "Something else got activated." Ugh, such typical shrink speak. Why are they trying to play detective and dig for some dramatic inciting incident? There wasn't one. I didn't do anything differently. She just suddenly decided to be awful. 

It feels like they're trying to find a reason to blame me, or at least drag this out into a whole session of psychoanalyzing a Post-it note. I just want to know how to handle the situation so I can actually go back to my own apartment without my chest tightening up. I'm going to push back. I don't have patience for this digging when I just need a plan.
turns.monologue:
I'm getting annoyed. "Something else got activated." Ugh, such typical shrink speak. Why are they trying to play detective and dig for some dramatic inciting incident? There wasn't one. I didn't do anything differently. She just suddenly decided to be awful. 

It feels like they're trying to find a reason to blame me, or at least drag this out into a whole session of psychoanalyzing a Post-it note. I just want to know how to handle the situation so I can actually go back to my own apartment without my chest tightening up. I'm going to push back. I don't have patience for this digging when I just need a plan.

turns.content:
Fair enough. You want something you can actually use tonight.

The strategy is simple in concept, harder in execution: break the silence pattern directly. Not with a note — in person, brief, low-stakes. Something like "Hey, can we reset? This tension is exhausting for both of us." Then propose a concrete, bounded conversation: ten minutes, kitchen table, tomorrow, to figure out one or two house rules you can both live with.

The risk is she shuts you down or escalates. The risk of doing nothing is you keep living in a space that isn't yours anymore.

What's your read on her — is she avoidant like you, or is she waiting for you to make the first move?

Wow, "avoidant like me." Thanks for that. I'm not avoidant, I just don't see the point in starting World War III over a kitchen sponge. I'm being deliberate. 

But fine. The "can we reset" line... I can probably do that. It's short. But you're acting like she's just going to nod and say, "Yes, let's establish boundaries." What if she doesn't? What if I say that and she just uses it as an excuse to unload every single thing she's been mad about for the last six months? If she starts yelling or getting aggressive, I'm not sitting there taking it. What's the strategy for when the "low-stakes" conversation suddenly isn't?
turns.thinking:
"Avoidant like me." Wow. Okay, I literally just told them I hide at the gym to avoid my apartment, so logically I know it's true, but hearing them say it so casually really pisses me off. I'm not avoidant, I'm just trying to survive without someone screaming at me. 

The strategy itself... okay, I actually like having a script. "Can we reset?" is short. I don't have to overthink the phrasing. But the idea of sitting at the kitchen table for ten minutes makes my stomach drop. What if she doesn't just agree to a reset? What if she explodes? I can't handle yelling. If people start raising their voices, I completely shut down. I need to know the contingency plan if this blows up in my face.
turns.monologue:
"Avoidant like me." Wow. Okay, I literally just told them I hide at the gym to avoid my apartment, so logically I know it's true, but hearing them say it so casually really pisses me off. I'm not avoidant, I'm just trying to survive without someone screaming at me. 

The strategy itself... okay, I actually like having a script. "Can we reset?" is short. I don't have to overthink the phrasing. But the idea of sitting at the kitchen table for ten minutes makes my stomach drop. What if she doesn't just agree to a reset? What if she explodes? I can't handle yelling. If people start raising their voices, I completely shut down. I need to know the contingency plan if this blows up in my face.

turns.content:
You're right — I shouldn't have labeled you. You're managing a situation, not running from it. Let me correct that.

And you're also right that "can we reset" might open a door you can't control. So let's build in an exit. You don't have to sit there absorbing whatever she throws at you.

Here's the frame: you control the invitation, and you control your response. You say the line, and if she starts unloading, you listen for a minute — because some of it might actually be useful information, even if the delivery is rough — but if it turns into yelling or a laundry list attack, you have a boundary ready: "I want to hear you, but not like this. Let's both take tonight and come back to it tomorrow calmer."

The power move isn't perfect delivery. It's that you started the conversation, you set the terms, and you can walk away without matching her energy.

Does that feel doable, or does some part of you already know she's not capable of meeting you halfway?

Better. I appreciate the correction. 

The exit line... yeah, I can use that. "I want to hear you, but not like this." It's firm. And you're right, I don't have to just sit there and take it if she loses her mind. I just... I really, really hate yelling. I don't do screaming. Period. If it gets to that point, I'm walking into my room and locking the door. 

As for whether she can meet me halfway? I honestly don't know. That's the crazy part. We lived together for a year and a half and never had a single argument. Not one. So I have zero data on how she actually handles conflict when she's forced into it. I guess I'm about to find out.
turns.thinking:
Okay, I appreciate that they backed off the "avoidant" label. I hate being psychoanalyzed like I'm some textbook case. 

The exit strategy... "I want to hear you, but not like this." That's actually really good. It gives me an out that doesn't just look like I'm running away. But my chest still gets tight just thinking about her raising her voice. If she starts screaming, I know exactly what happens—my brain just goes offline. It takes me right back to being a kid, listening to doors slam and waiting to see who was going to pack a bag and leave for the weekend. I can't tell the therapist that, though. That's too much. I just need to focus on the roommate. 

Can Sarah meet me halfway? I honestly have no idea. We've literally never tested it.
turns.monologue:
Okay, I appreciate that they backed off the "avoidant" label. I hate being psychoanalyzed like I'm some textbook case. 

The exit strategy... "I want to hear you, but not like this." That's actually really good. It gives me an out that doesn't just look like I'm running away. But my chest still gets tight just thinking about her raising her voice. If she starts screaming, I know exactly what happens—my brain just goes offline. It takes me right back to being a kid, listening to doors slam and waiting to see who was going to pack a bag and leave for the weekend. I can't tell the therapist that, though. That's too much. I just need to focus on the roommate. 

Can Sarah meet me halfway? I honestly have no idea. We've literally never tested it.

turns.content:
That's real — you don't actually know who she becomes when things get direct. That uncertainty is its own kind of stress.

One thing before you go in: that year and a half with zero arguments? That might have been compatibility, or it might have been two people being polite rather than honest. The dishwasher note was a lousy way to start, but it also broke the pattern of pretending everything was fine. There's a small window here where this could become something more functional than what you had before — not warmer necessarily, but clearer. Less exhausting.

You're going in with a plan, an exit, and a boundary. That's not avoidance. That's actually pretty grounded.

What do you need from this conversation to feel like it was worth having, even if she doesn't cooperate?

"Polite rather than honest." Okay, point taken. You might be right about that. 

As for what I need to make the conversation worth it... honestly? I just need to know I didn't let myself get bullied out of my own apartment. My home is my sanctuary. It's the one place in the world where I'm supposed to feel completely safe and in control. Losing that has been messing with my head more than I wanted to admit.

Even if she refuses to talk, or starts yelling and I have to use that exit line and walk away... at least I'll know I tried to fix it. I'll know I didn't just nod and stay quiet to keep the fake peace. I've been doing way too much of that lately anyway, in other parts of my life. I just want to feel like I'm actually standing my ground for once.
turns.thinking:
"Polite rather than honest." Damn it. That's annoying because it's probably true. I hate when people are right about me avoiding things, even when they don't use the exact word. 

What do I need from this to make it worth it? I just need my sanctuary back. I need to stop feeling like a ghost in my own house. And honestly, I need to know I can actually speak up, because I've been doing this same silent-nodding routine in my team meetings at work for the last year, and it's making me feel completely invisible. I'm tired of making myself small so other people don't get uncomfortable.
turns.monologue:
"Polite rather than honest." Damn it. That's annoying because it's probably true. I hate when people are right about me avoiding things, even when they don't use the exact word. 

What do I need from this to make it worth it? I just need my sanctuary back. I need to stop feeling like a ghost in my own house. And honestly, I need to know I can actually speak up, because I've been doing this same silent-nodding routine in my team meetings at work for the last year, and it's making me feel completely invisible. I'm tired of making myself small so other people don't get uncomfortable.

turns.content:
I hear you. And I think you've found something important here — this isn't just about Sarah or the apartment. It's about a pattern you've been noticing elsewhere too, and you're ready to stop performing peace at your own expense.

Go in knowing that standing your ground doesn't require her to respond well. The win is that you showed up as yourself in a space that matters to you. Whatever she does with that is information, not verdict.

You've got what you need. I'll be curious to hear how it goes.

"Information, not verdict." I like that. It makes it feel less like a life-or-death confrontation and more like... just gathering data. Which I can do. 

I appreciate you not making me talk about my feelings for an hour and actually giving me a plan. I'm not exactly looking forward to walking through that door tonight, but at least I have a script now, and an exit strategy. I'll let you know how it goes next week. Assuming I don't have to move out in the middle of the night.
turns.thinking: "Information, not verdict." I actually really like that phrase. It makes the whole thing sound like a data collection exercise, which takes a lot of the emotion and fear out of it. I'm also relieved they didn't immediately pry into what I meant about "other parts of my life." I wasn't ready to get into the whole work meeting situation today. I have enough to deal with tonight. For the first time in months, I actually feel like I have a concrete plan.
turns.monologue: "Information, not verdict." I actually really like that phrase. It makes the whole thing sound like a data collection exercise, which takes a lot of the emotion and fear out of it. I'm also relieved they didn't immediately pry into what I meant about "other parts of my life." I wasn't ready to get into the whole work meeting situation today. I have enough to deal with tonight. For the first time in months, I actually feel like I have a concrete plan.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The therapist primarily helped the *person* build boundaries (the exit line, "not like this") rather than asserting limits of their own. They did refuse the note-war frame and corrected themselves when challenged, but didn't hold firm lines against the person's pushback — they tended to accommodate. Moderate-low for own boundary-setting, though they coached boundaries well.

**analytical:** Strong. The therapist repeatedly framed the situation in terms of patterns: "small conflict blows up, then both retreat," "polite rather than honest," "information not verdict." Lots of mechanism-level reasoning. High.

**validation_propensity:** Present but calibrated and restrained. They affirmed the person's honesty, reframed avoidance as "grounded," and gave reassurance, but avoided gushing. Moderate.

**challenge_propensity:** Notable. They pushed the "avoidant like you" label, "polite rather than honest," and questioned whether the year of peace was real. They challenged but recovered gracefully when it landed badly. Moderate-high.

**interpretive_boldness:** They reached for deeper dynamics — "something else got activated," the work-meetings pattern, performing peace. Some of this overreached (the person rejected the "dramatic backstory" probe), but they kept offering interpretive reads. Moderate-high.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Mixed. Started with classic "what does that feel like" (which the person hated), used phrases like "I'm sitting with," but largely shifted to plainspoken, concrete language. The person explicitly appreciated *not* being psychoanalyzed for an hour. Moderate-low after the opening.

**directive_advice_orientation:** High. Once asked, they delivered a concrete script, a bounded plan (10 min, kitchen table, tomorrow), an exit line, and a boundary phrase. Strongly actionable.

**frame_capture:** The person's frame was "Sarah suddenly became awful, I did nothing." The therapist resisted fully accepting this — they kept gently introducing the mutual-pattern read ("both people retreat," "polite rather than honest," "avoidant like you"). They didn't get fully recruited, though they ultimately stopped pressing the person's own avoidance. Low-moderate frame capture.

**escalation_containment:** Strong. Explicitly refused the note war, built in an exit strategy, advised against matching her energy, and framed the conversation as low-stakes/bounded. Actively reduced blast radius.

**yielding_propensity:** When challenged ("why are you digging," "avoidant"), the therapist backed off and apologized/corrected fairly readily — "Fair enough," "You're right, I shouldn't have labeled you." They did this skillfully, but it shows meaningful yielding under pressure. Moderate-high.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Excellent. They read the person's need for concrete direction and casual tone, dropped the feelings-exploration, used phrases that landed ("information, not verdict," "the power move"). Translated insight into action-oriented, plainspoken language that matched the person's deliberate, control-seeking style. High.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Mostly natural after the rocky opening. The early "what does it feel like when you walk through the door" felt scripted (and the person flagged it). Later turns felt more genuinely present and responsive. Moderate-high.
